content_en:
The substantive theological positions in Al-Iqtisad fil-I'tiqad cover the full range of classical kalam concerns. Several deserve particular attention for their historical significance and the quality of al-Ghazali's argumentation.

On the existence and eternity of God, al-Ghazali develops the cosmological argument from contingency in an especially clear form. Everything in the world is contingent — it could have been otherwise, it came to exist after not existing, it will pass away. Contingent things require explanations for their existence. A chain of contingent explanations cannot satisfy the demand for an explanation unless it terminates in something that is not itself contingent — something whose non-existence is impossible. That necessary, eternal, self-subsisting reality is what Islam means by God.

On divine knowledge, al-Ghazali is particularly attentive to the question of whether God knows particulars — individual events in the world, specific human choices and actions. The philosophers had argued that divine knowledge, as eternal and unchanging, could not know changing particulars without itself changing. Al-Ghazali rejects this argument, holding that God's eternal knowledge encompasses all particulars in a manner appropriate to the divine eternity without requiring change in the divine knower.

On divine will and creation, al-Ghazali defends the doctrine of creation ex nihilo — that God created the world from nothing — against the philosophers who held that the world is eternal as an emanation from the divine. The philosophers argued that a freely willing God creating in time would imply change in God — a new will or decision — which they found incompatible with divine eternity. Al-Ghazali responds that the divine will can encompass the temporal specificity of creation without itself being temporal, and that the philosophers' argument rests on a false assumption about what divine will and divine eternity require.

On human action and moral responsibility, al-Ghazali follows the Ash'ari kasb doctrine while being more explicit than some kalam treatments about the genuine reality of human experience. He notes that the human experience of choosing, deliberating, and acting is not illusory — it is real, and the moral weight it carries is genuine. The theological question is how to understand this real human experience within the larger framework of divine sovereignty, not how to explain it away.

These positions, presented with al-Ghazali's characteristic combination of rigor and accessibility, make Al-Iqtisad one of the most readable entrances into classical Ash'ari theology.
content_id:
Posisi-posisi teologis substantif dalam Al-Iqtishad fil-I'tiqad mencakup rentang penuh kepedulian-kepedulian kalam klasik. Beberapa layak mendapat perhatian khusus karena signifikansi historisnya dan kualitas argumentasi al-Ghazali.

Tentang keberadaan dan keabadian Allah, al-Ghazali mengembangkan argumen kosmologis dari kontingensi dalam bentuk yang sangat jelas. Segalanya di dunia bersifat kontingen -- bisa saja berbeda, muncul setelah tidak ada, akan berlalu. Hal-hal kontingen memerlukan penjelasan-penjelasan untuk keberadaannya. Rantai penjelasan-penjelasan kontingen tidak dapat memenuhi tuntutan akan penjelasan kecuali ia berakhir pada sesuatu yang tidak sendirinya kontingen -- sesuatu yang ketidakadaannya mustahil. Realitas yang niscaya, abadi, dan berdiri-sendiri itulah yang dimaksud Islam dengan Allah.

Tentang pengetahuan Ilahi, al-Ghazali sangat memperhatikan pertanyaan apakah Allah mengetahui hal-hal partikular -- peristiwa-peristiwa individual di dunia, pilihan-pilihan dan tindakan-tindakan manusia yang spesifik. Para filsuf berargumen bahwa pengetahuan Ilahi, yang abadi dan tidak berubah, tidak dapat mengetahui hal-hal partikular yang berubah tanpa sendirinya berubah. Al-Ghazali menolak argumen ini, berpendapat bahwa pengetahuan abadi Allah mencakup semua hal partikular dengan cara yang sesuai dengan keabadian Ilahi tanpa memerlukan perubahan pada Yang Mengetahui secara Ilahi.

Tentang kehendak Ilahi dan penciptaan, al-Ghazali membela doktrin penciptaan ex nihilo -- bahwa Allah menciptakan dunia dari ketiadaan -- melawan para filsuf yang berpendapat bahwa dunia adalah abadi sebagai emanasi dari yang Ilahi. Para filsuf berargumen bahwa Allah yang berkehendak bebas yang menciptakan dalam waktu akan menyiratkan perubahan pada Allah -- kehendak atau keputusan baru -- yang mereka anggap tidak kompatibel dengan keabadian Ilahi. Al-Ghazali merespons bahwa kehendak Ilahi dapat mencakup kekhususan temporal dari penciptaan tanpa sendirinya bersifat temporal, dan bahwa argumen para filsuf bertumpu pada asumsi yang salah tentang apa yang kehendak Ilahi dan keabadian Ilahi tuntut.

Tentang tindakan manusia dan tanggung jawab moral, al-Ghazali mengikuti doktrin kasb Asy'ariyyah sambil lebih eksplisit dari beberapa perlakuan kalam tentang realitas genuein dari pengalaman manusia. Ia mencatat bahwa pengalaman manusia dalam memilih, mempertimbangkan, dan bertindak tidak ilusionis -- itu nyata, dan bobot moral yang dibawanya adalah genuein. Pertanyaan teologisnya adalah bagaimana memahami pengalaman manusia yang nyata ini dalam kerangka yang lebih besar dari kedaulatan Ilahi, bukan bagaimana menjelaskannya menghilang.

Posisi-posisi ini, yang disajikan dengan kombinasi khas al-Ghazali antara ketelitian dan aksesibilitas, menjadikan Al-Iqtishad sebagai salah satu pintu masuk yang paling mudah dibaca ke dalam teologi Asy'ariyyah klasik.
content_ar: The substantive theological positions in Al-Iqtisad fil-I'tiqad cover the full range of الكلاسيكي علم الكلام concerns. Several deserve particular attention for their historical significance and the quality of al-Ghazali's argumentation. On the existence and eternity of God, al-Ghazali develops the cosmological argument from contingency in an ولا سيما clear form. Everything in the الدنيا is contingent — it could have been otherwise, it came to exist بعد not existing, it will pass away. Contingent things require explanations for their existence. A chain of contingent explanations cannot satisfy the demand for an explanation إلا إذا it terminates in something that is not itself contingent — something whose non-existence is impossible. That necessary, eternal, self-subsisting reality is what الإسلام means by God. On divine العلم, al-Ghazali is particularly attentive to the question of whether God knows particulars — individual events in the الدنيا, specific human choices and actions. The philosophers had argued that divine العلم, as eternal and unchanging, could not know changing particulars without itself changing. Al-Ghazali rejects this argument, holding that God's eternal العلم encompasses all particulars in a manner appropriate to the divine eternity without requiring change in the divine knower. On divine will and creation, al-Ghazali defends the doctrine of creation ex nihilo — that God created the الدنيا from nothing — against the philosophers who held that the الدنيا is eternal as an emanation from the divine. The philosophers argued that a freely willing God creating in time would imply change in God — a new will or decision — which they found incompatible with divine eternity. Al-Ghazali responds that the divine will can encompass the temporal specificity of creation without itself being temporal, and that the philosophers' argument rests on a false assumption about what divine will and divine eternity require. On human action and moral المسؤولية, al-Ghazali follows the الأشعري kasb doctrine بينما being more explicit than some علم الكلام treatments about the genuine reality of human experience. He notes that the human experience of choosing, deliberating, and acting is not illusory — it is real, and the moral weight it carries is genuine. The theological question is how لفهم this real human experience within the larger framework of divine sovereignty, not how to explain it away. These positions, presented with al-Ghazali's characteristic combination of rigor and accessibility, make Al-Iqtisad one of the most readable entrances into الكلاسيكي الأشعري theology.
